rationale: The citation is used in a theory-building passage, not just a background list. The authors summarize Dhar, Huber, and Khan (2007) as showing that an implemental mind-set after an initial purchase increases subsequent purchasing, then explicitly connect this to their own attention-and-partition account. They use it to argue that partitions may interrupt that implemental state by reintroducing deliberation, which gives the cited paper a meaningful conceptual role.
relation_description: The citing paper uses the shopping momentum effect as a related mechanism account of sequential purchasing and then proposes that partition-induced deliberation can interrupt that implemental mind-set. This positions the citing paper as conceptually qualifying when the cited effect should persist.
